ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for Thai-style spicy sour steamed clams specifically; closest parent 'clam food product' (FOODON_03302757) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJM covers cooked clams/cockles as closest parent; no specific Thai-style preparation code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for Thai-style spicy sour steamed clams.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of steamed clams in Thai-style spicy and sour sauce is approximately 350 g, representing a shared seafood dish for one to two persons at a Zi Char or Malaysian seafood restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Clams, mixed species, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15159) combined with sauce component estimation from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core clam macros derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked clams (NDB 15159 / FDC ID 175177). Sauce components (chilli, lemongrass, fish sauce, lime juice, garlic, shallots) contribute additional sodium, minor carbohydrate, and sugar. Composite per-100g values are estimated by weighting approximately 70% cooked clam meat and 30% sauce/broth by mass, consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA methodology for shellfish dishes. Sodium is elevated due to fish sauce contribution.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — shellfish and seafood dishes; analogous estimate based on cooked shellfish / mixed seafood dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai-style spicy sour steamed clams. GI estimated at 20 based on analogous cooked shellfish and mixed seafood dishes reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Shellfish dishes with minimal starch and moderate protein have consistently low GI values (range 15–30). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (20 × 20.3) / 100 ≈ 4.1, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving ≈ 20.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and shellfish broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce, as a 350 g serving provides approximately 1820 mg sodium — exceeding 75% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed clams in Thai-style spicy sour sauce have a low GI (estimated 20) and a low glycemic load per serving (~4.1), making this dish suitable for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium intake is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium and moderate cholesterol offset by low saturated fat and omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the dish is low in saturated fat and provides cardioprotective omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids from clam meat, the high sodium content (~520 mg/100 g) warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease who require strict sodium restriction.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Clams in Thai-Style Spicy and Sour Sauce is a popular seafood dish served at Malaysian and Singaporean Zi Char restaurants, featuring live or fresh clams (commonly lala or hard-shell clams) steamed and then tossed in a vibrant Thai-inspired sauce made from fresh red chillies, lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, fish sauce, lime juice, garlic, shallots, and Thai basil. The dish delivers a bold combination of spicy heat, sharp sourness, and savoury umami depth, with the clam meat remaining tender and juicy. Texturally, the clams are soft and briny, contrasting with the aromatic, slightly oily sauce. Nutritionally, clams are an exceptional source of vitamin B12 (approximately 22 mcg per 100 g, far exceeding daily requirements), iron (approximately 14 mg per 100 g), selenium, and lean protein (approximately 14.2 g per 100 g), while remaining low in fat and calories. The dish has a low glycemic index (estimated GI 20) and low glycemic load, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. However, the sodium content is high due to fish sauce, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. This dish is gluten-free and dairy-free but contains shellfish and fish allergens. It is commonly ordered as a shared dish at Zi Char stalls and seafood restaurants in Singapore and Malaysia, and reflects the cross-cultural Thai culinary influence prevalent in the region.
common_user_queries: Is steamed clams in Thai spicy sour sauce healthy?
How many calories in Thai style steamed clams?
Is this dish high in sodium?
Is lala in Thai sauce gluten free?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai-style spicy sour steamed clams exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked clam data (a well-validated approved secondary source) with sauce component estimation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database shellfish data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked clams (NDB 15159) and is not reliably quantified in the sauce components. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked clams; naturally zero in the plant-based sauce components). Sodium value of 520 mg/100g reflects the composite dish including fish sauce contribution; plain cooked clam meat alone contains approximately 95 mg/100g sodium per USDA SR Legacy. Iron value of 14 mg/100g reflects the well-documented high iron content of cooked clams per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin B12 value of 22 mcg/100g reflects the exceptionally high B12 content of cooked clams, consistent with USDA SR Legacy data. Halal status is marked false as fish sauce of uncertain halal certification is a standard ingredient; consumers should verify with the restaurant. GI estimated at 20 based on analogous cooked shellfish from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement available for this specific preparation.

improvement_actions_taken: Expanded food_name to canonical form 'Steamed Clams in Thai-Style Spicy and Sour Sauce'.
Added mandatory multilingual names: Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi (Singaporean/Malaysian cuisine requirement), plus Thai script given Thai culinary influence.
Derived per_100g nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked clams (FDC 175177) with sauce component weighting per FAO/INFOODS SEA methodology.
Set GI to 20 (analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database shellfish data) with full source documentation.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 in notes as not reported in source.
Documented trans_fat_g = 0.0 as confirmed zero in notes.
Verified all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Set ontology match_type values exclusively to permitted values: closest_parent and none.
Provided specific clinical guidance for all three health conditions.
